title: Fusion Retrieval (Hybrid Dense + BM25 with Weighted Score Fusion)
one_liner: Runs vector similarity search and BM25 keyword search over the same chunks, normalizes both score lists, and merges them with a tunable weighted sum to rank documents.
problem_solved: Pure vector search misses exact keyword/term matches (names, codes, jargon, acronyms) while pure BM25 misses semantically related passages that use different wording; fusion retrieval combines both signals so a single retriever handles both query styles.
failure_modes: My retriever misses documents that contain the exact term I searched for because embeddings smooth over rare words, product codes, IDs, or acronyms
Keyword search fails when users phrase questions differently from the document wording (vocabulary mismatch / synonyms)
Retrieval quality is inconsistent across query types: some queries need exact matching, others need semantic understanding
Domain-specific jargon or entities are poorly represented in the embedding model, so semantically 'close' but wrong chunks are retrieved
A single retrieval method returns plausible-looking but off-target chunks, polluting the context window and causing wrong answers
when_to_use: Your corpus mixes natural prose with exact-match-critical tokens: legal citations, part numbers, API names, medical codes, people/product names
Users issue both keyword-style queries and full natural-language questions against the same index
You observe embedding-only retrieval ranking topically similar but wrong chunks above chunks containing the literal answer term
You want a cheap, no-extra-LLM-call retrieval quality boost before reaching for rerankers or query rewriting
You need a tunable dial (alpha) to shift between semantic and lexical matching per use case or per query class
tradeoffs: Two indexes to build, store, and keep in sync (FAISS vector store + BM25 index over identical chunks)

The notebook's implementation scores the ENTIRE corpus per query (similarity_search with k=ntotal) to align score arrays, which is O(N) per query and does not scale beyond small corpora; production systems should use top-k union + Reciprocal Rank Fusion or a vector DB with native hybrid search instead
alpha (vector vs BM25 weight) is a hyperparameter that needs tuning per corpus/query mix; a bad alpha can underperform either method alone
Min-max score normalization is brittle: scores from the two systems are not calibrated, and outlier scores compress the rest of the distribution
Naive whitespace tokenization for BM25 (query.split()) misses stemming, casing, and punctuation handling, weakening the lexical signal
Adds no benefit if your queries are purely semantic paraphrases or purely exact-match lookups — one method alone would suffice and be simpler

Caveat in this reference code: vector results are returned sorted by similarity, not by corpus index order, so aligning them positionally with BM25 scores is fragile — verify score-to-document alignment when adapting it
how_it_works: At indexing time, a PDF is loaded (PyPDFLoader), split with RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter (chunk_size=1000, overlap=200), and cleaned; the identical chunk list feeds two indexes: a FAISS vector store built from OpenAIEmbeddings, and a BM25Okapi index built from whitespace-tokenized chunk text. At query time, fusion_retrieval(vectorstore, bm25, query, k, alpha) (1) fetches all documents from the vector store, (2) computes BM25 scores for the whitespace-split query over all chunks, (3) runs similarity_search_with_score over all chunks and inverts FAISS distances into similarities (1 - min-max normalized distance), (4) min-max normalizes BM25 scores, (5) combines them as alpha * vector_scores + (1 - alpha) * bm25_scores, (6) argsorts descending and returns the top-k Documents. alpha=0.5 weights both equally; raise it for more semantic behavior, lower it for more lexical behavior. The technique spans indexing (dual index build) but primarily operates at retrieval. No LLM calls are made anywhere in the retrieval path — costs are embedding calls at index time only.
key_dependencies: langchain (PyPDFLoader, RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter, FAISS vectorstore wrapper, Document)
key_code_signals: vector_scores = 1 - (vector_scores - min) / (max - min + epsilon)  # distance-to-similarity inversion
related_techniques: simple_rag: prerequisite baseline that fusion retrieval extends with a second lexical index
reranking: composes — a cross-encoder/LLM reranker can re-order fusion's fused candidate list; also an alternative route to fixing ranking quality
query_transformations: alternative for vocabulary mismatch — rewrites the query instead of adding a lexical index
hyde_hypothetical_document_embedding: alternative that bridges query-document semantic gap on the embedding side
dartboard: alternative advanced retrieval that optimizes combined relevance-and-diversity instead of lexical-semantic fusion
contextual_compression: composes downstream — compress the fused top-k before generation
multi_faceted_filtering: composes — apply metadata/score filters on top of fused results
summary: Fusion retrieval is hybrid search: index the same text chunks twice — once in a FAISS vector store with OpenAI embeddings for semantic similarity, once in a BM25Okapi index for keyword relevance — then at query time score all chunks with both methods, min-max normalize each score list (inverting FAISS distances into similarities), and rank by a weighted sum alpha * vector + (1 - alpha) * bm25, returning top-k. It fixes the complementary blind spots of each retriever: embeddings miss exact tokens like product codes, names, IDs, and acronyms, while BM25 misses paraphrases and synonym-heavy queries. It requires no extra LLM calls, so query latency cost is low, but you maintain two indexes and must tune alpha. The reference implementation scores the entire corpus per query to align score arrays, which only works for small corpora; production variants should fuse top-k lists (e.g., Reciprocal Rank Fusion) or use a vector database with native hybrid search. Best used as a cheap first upgrade over vanilla dense retrieval, optionally followed by a reranker.
notes_for_chunking: Well-structured, small notebook (~22 cells). Cell 0 is a book advertisement (junk — strip). Cell 1 is a long, self-contained markdown overview (Overview/Motivation/Key Components/Method Details/Benefits/Conclusion) that makes an excellent standalone chunk. Cells 7-9 are data download boilerplate (strip wget). The core content is three function-defining code cells, each preceded by a short but accurate '###' markdown header: encode_pdf_and_get_split_documents (indexing), create_bm25_index, and fusion_retrieval (the key cell, with a docstring and numbered step comments — ideal chunk on its own).
